hlrs.title: Natural Language Input to Structured Process Representation
hlrs.description: The system must accept a free-form natural language description of a process and extract a structured representation comprising steps, decision points, roles or actors, and flow sequences. The structured representation must be sufficient to drive diagram rendering without further user intervention.
hlrs.rationale: This is the core differentiating capability — the NL-to-structure layer that existing tools do not provide, and upon which all rendering and editing depends.
hlrs.impact_if_omitted: The product is indistinguishable from a conventional diagram editor and the primary differentiator does not exist.

hlrs.title: Visual Process Diagram Rendering from Structured Data
hlrs.description: The system must render the structured process representation as a visual process map in the browser, using standard process notation including swimlanes, decision diamonds, and flow arrows.
hlrs.rationale: Addresses the primary user goal of producing a usable, publication-quality diagram without manual layout work.
hlrs.impact_if_omitted: The tool produces structured data with no human-readable output, and the core user value — a diagram you can hand to a dev team or include in a document — is not delivered.

hlrs.title: Direct Visual Diagram Editing with Representation Sync
hlrs.description: The system must allow users to edit the rendered diagram through direct manipulation — reordering steps, renaming nodes, adding and removing steps — and must update the underlying structured representation to reflect each change.
hlrs.rationale: Addresses the maintenance friction identified in the problem statement: processes change, and users must be able to update a map without starting over.
hlrs.impact_if_omitted: The tool is useful for initial generation only; iterative refinement reverts users to the manual overhead of conventional tools.

hlrs.title: Natural Language Instructions for Diagram Modification
hlrs.description: The system must accept natural language instructions that modify an existing diagram — such as inserting a step between two existing steps or reassigning an actor — and must update both the visual diagram and the underlying structured representation accordingly.
hlrs.rationale: Preserves the natural language interaction model as the primary editing interface, consistent with the tool's AI-first positioning and its design for verbal thinkers.
hlrs.impact_if_omitted: Users who prefer natural language editing are forced to switch to visual manipulation for changes, undermining the core interaction model and differentiator.

hlrs.title: Export to PNG, SVG, and Structured JSON
hlrs.description: The system must support export of a completed process map in at least three formats: PNG, SVG, and a structured JSON representation of the process data.
hlrs.rationale: Addresses both primary user needs (embeddable diagrams for documents and handoffs) and secondary user needs (developers and technical writers requiring machine-readable process data).
hlrs.impact_if_omitted: Maps exist only within the tool; the design document and developer handoff use cases explicitly cited as the standard of useful output are not met.

hlrs.title: Ambiguous or Contradictory Input Detection and Handling
hlrs.description: The system must detect when a natural language description is ambiguous, underspecified, or contradictory, and must respond by prompting the user for clarification or rendering a partial map with explicit markers on unresolved sections, rather than silently producing a diagram that misrepresents the described process.
hlrs.rationale: Ambiguity in NL input is identified in the concept as a known risk; a silently wrong process map handed to a dev team or included in documentation is worse than no map.
hlrs.impact_if_omitted: The tool produces misleading diagrams from poorly-specified input, trust in outputs degrades, and the publication-quality claim cannot be sustained.

hlrs.title: Browser-Native Zero-Install Access
hlrs.description: The system shall be fully accessible in a standard browser without software installation or mandatory account creation. Fast time-to-first-map is a quality target — the tool should feel responsive — but output quality and correctness take precedence over raw latency.
hlrs.rationale: Zero-install access is an explicitly stated differentiator over conventional tools, and frictionless access is central to casual evaluation and word-of-mouth adoption.
hlrs.impact_if_omitted: The friction advantage over existing tools is removed and the frictionless-trial adoption model is not viable.

hlrs.title: NL Parsing and Rendering Latency as a Quality Target
hlrs.description: The system shall treat response latency as a quality characteristic to optimise for, not a hard constraint. Backend and infrastructure choices should favour fast NL parsing and rendering, with the understanding that output accuracy and completeness take precedence when they are in tension with speed.
hlrs.rationale: Fast results are part of the intended user experience, but the concept note is explicit that quality matters more than speed — responsiveness should be a design consideration, not a ceiling the product compromises correctness to meet.
hlrs.impact_if_omitted: Without any latency guidance, backend architecture decisions may not treat responsiveness as a quality dimension at all, producing a tool that is both slow and accurate when a reasonable balance is achievable.

hlrs.title: Stable, Documented JSON Export Schema
hlrs.description: The system must produce a JSON export that conforms to a documented, stable schema representing steps, decision points, actors, and flow sequences in a form consumable by external tools without bespoke parsing.
hlrs.rationale: The secondary user audience — developers and technical writers — requires reliable machine-readable output; an undocumented or unstable schema limits interoperability.
hlrs.impact_if_omitted: The JSON export functions as an opaque data dump rather than an integration point, and the downstream tool consumption use case is not reliably achievable.
